title: 6 Selling Canadian Dollar Money Orders
body_text:
6	Selling Canadian Dollar Money Orders
6.1	Overview of Selling Canadian Dollar Money Orders
Money Orders are like cash so it is very important that you practice due diligence when selling Money Orders. Always obtain payment before giving the Money Order to the purchaser.
The following images are samples of the Money Orders available to the BC and Ontario credit unions. See Figure 1 and Figure 2.
Figure 1: Sample Money Order, BC Credit Unions
Figure 2: Sample Money Order, Ontario Credit Unions
6.2	Selling Canadian Dollar Money Orders
As with other transactions, ensure that
PCMLTFA
requirements are met regarding identification and record keeping, reporting (e.g., terrorist property reporting, large cash transaction reporting, suspicious transaction reporting), and records retention. Contact your AML Compliance Officer or FINTRAC for more information. Credit unions that subscribe to the Operations Manual Program can also refer to the PCMLTFA Compliance Part.
Caution
:	Donât leave blank Money Orders unattended on the counter. Keep the Money Order in your possession until the purchaser has paid for it.
Proceed as follows to sell a Canadian dollar Money Order:
Identify the purchaser and record transaction details. If the Money Order is $3,000 (Canadian equivalent) or more, the credit union must ascertain the identity of the purchaser. This identification requirement also applies if two or more Money Orders totaling $3,000 (Canadian Equivalent) or more are issued. If the transaction is $3,000 (Canadian Equivalent) or more, the credit union must also record the name and address of the individual, the date of the transaction, the amount, and the medium of exchange (e.g., cash, cheque, travelers cheques, Money Orders).
Note:	If the information that must be recorded about the transaction is on the transaction slip or other account documents (e.g., Signature Card), a separate record doesnât need to be kept. Also, the credit union is exempt from the client identification requirement if the individual authorizing the transaction has signed a Signature Card for an account, or is authorized to act regarding such an account, the credit union employee conducting the transaction recognizes the individual, and there is no doubt about the identification information previously collected.
Obtain payment from the purchaser. Ensure that the funds are guaranteed or cleared before the Money Order is given to the purchaser. If the purchaser is a member, debit the memberâs account. Credit your internal Money Orders account (i.e. the account number that is printed on the MICR line of the Money Orders).
Note:	Additional requirements apply if a Money Order is paid for with $10,000 (Canadian equivalent) or more in cash. Contact your AML Compliance Officer or FINTRAC for more information. Credit unions that subscribe to the Operations Manual Program can also refer to the PCMLTFA Compliance Part.
Record the current date, the dollar value, and the amount in figures on the Money Order. Use an imprinter to record the dollar amount on the face of the Money Order to prevent it from being fraudulently altered.
Important
:	Donât exceed the maximum amount recorded on the face of the Money Order. If necessary, issue multiple Money Orders that add up to the required amount. If you accidentally record an amount that is more than the maximum dollar value on the face of the Money Order, void it immediately. See Section 7.2, Voiding Canadian Dollar Money Orders.
Instruct the purchaser to record the payeeâs name, sign the Money Order, and record the purchaserâs address. It is a good practice to insist that the payeeâs name be entered on the Money Order before the purchaser leaves the credit union.
Note:	The credit union is required to keep sufficient information about the purchaser to locate that person if the Money Order isnât cashed. If the purchaser isnât a member, it is recommended that the credit union record additional contact information on its copy of the Money Order.
Distribute the copies of the Money Order as follows:
â
Original
â Give to the purchaser. The purchaser assumes responsibility for delivering the Money Order to the payee. Encourage the purchaser to send the Money Order by registered mail or courier.
â
Copy 2
â Give to the purchaser as a receipt.
â
Copy 3
â File in the Outstanding Money Order file and use this copy to reconcile cleared Money Orders. See Section 8.2, Reconciling Canadian Dollar Money Orders, for more information.
â
Copy 4
â Retain for your records.
Related Information
Section 7.2
, Voiding Canadian Dollar Money Orders
Section 8.2
, Reconciling Canadian Dollar Money Orders
END
